schools.summary: Paul turns to defend and describe his ministry, appealing to their own memory: 'yourselves, brethren, know our entrance in (our coming, our visit) unto you' — they themselves could testify to the character of his work; 'that it was not in vain (kene — empty, fruitless, void of result)' — not hollow or powerless but full of effect, his ministry having borne real fruit in their conversion (as ch. 1 showed); the very existence of the church proved his coming was not empty (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: yourselves, brethren, know our entrance in unto you, that it was not in vain
Paul's ministry not in vain
the fruitful entrance
the church itself the proof

schools.summary: Paul recalls the ill-treatment that preceded his coming: 'we had suffered before, and were shamefully entreated (hybristhentes — insulted, outraged) at Philippi' — the beating and imprisonment there (Acts 16:22-24), a public indignity to Roman citizens; yet 'we were bold (eparresiasametha — waxed bold, took courage) in our God to speak unto you the gospel of God' — not intimidated by recent suffering, Paul preached boldly, his courage drawn from God; 'with much contention (en pollo agoni — conflict, struggle, amid great opposition)' (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).

themes: after that we had suffered before, and were shamefully entreated... at Philippi
we were bold in our God to speak unto you the gospel of God with much contention
boldness to preach despite suffering
courage drawn from God

schools.summary: Paul defends the integrity of his message: 'our exhortation (our preaching, appeal) was not of deceit (planes — error, delusion, no false doctrine), nor of uncleanness (akatharsias — impure motive or aim), nor in guile (dolo — trickery, craftiness)' — his preaching was sound in doctrine, pure in motive, and honest in method; 'deceit' meaning error or delusion, his message being the truth of God, no fable or falsehood; springing from no impure motive (such as greed or lust, common to the false teachers of the age) and using no guile to win followers (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).

themes: our exhortation was not of deceit, nor of uncleanness, nor in guile
the purity of Paul's preaching
free of deceit, impure motive, and guile
sound in doctrine, pure in aim

schools.summary: Paul's sense of trust: 'as we were allowed (dedokimasmetha — approved, tested and found fit) of God to be put in trust with (entrusted with) the gospel, even so we speak' — God had examined and approved him, committing the gospel to his charge as a sacred trust; 'not as pleasing men, but God' — holding such a trust, Paul preached to please God, the one who entrusted him, not to win the favour of men, the steward answering to his Master not the crowd (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: he preaches to please 'God, which trieth (tests, proves, examines) our hearts' — the God who searches the inmost motives; Paul's aim is the approval of the One who sees the heart, not the applause of men who see only the outward.

themes: as we were allowed of God to be put in trust with the gospel, even so we speak
not as pleasing men, but God, which trieth our hearts
preaching to please God, not men
the God who tries the heart

schools.summary: Paul disclaims two faults of the false teacher: 'neither at any time used we flattering words (speech meant to please and win favour), as ye know' — he never fawned or flattered to gain influence; 'nor a cloke (prophasei — pretext, pretense, a covering) of covetousness' — he never used his ministry as a mask to conceal greed for gain; for the outward flattery he appeals to the Thessalonians ('as ye know'), for the hidden greed (which only God could see) to God ('God is witness') — the inward motive known to God alone (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: neither at any time used we flattering words, as ye know, nor a cloke of covetousness
God is witness
Paul's freedom from flattery and covetousness
pure motive, witnessed by them and God

schools.summary: The third disclaimer: 'nor of men sought we glory (honour, praise), neither of you, nor yet of others' — Paul did not preach to gain reputation or applause from anyone; 'when we might have been burdensome (en barei — claimed maintenance, or asserted our weight/authority), as the apostles of Christ' — Paul had the right, as an apostle, to be supported by the churches and to claim honour, but forewent it ('burdensome' meaning either claiming support or pressing apostolic authority), waiving his rights for the gospel's sake that he might be no charge to them (Barnes, Robertson, JFB, Wesley).

themes: nor of men sought we glory, neither of you, nor yet of others
when we might have been burdensome, as the apostles of Christ
not seeking glory though entitled to support
waiving his rights for the gospel

schools.summary: The received reading 'we were gentle (epioi — mild, kind, tender)' — Paul, who might have used apostolic authority (v.6), behaved with mildness; but the manuscripts strongly support 'we were babes (nepioi — little children),' differing by a single letter (the preceding word egenethemen ending in -n), read by the Vulgate, Ethiopic, and many ancient witnesses (so Gill: harmless, modest, disinterested as a little child). Either way, 'even as a nurse (trophos — a nursing mother) cherisheth (thalpe — warms, fosters, tenderly nurtures) her children' — Paul cared for the Thessalonians with the warm, self-giving tenderness of a mother nursing her own babes (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: the apostle, far from lording it over them, nourished them as a mother her infant; the maternal image marks the tenderness and patience with which he fed them the milk of the word.

debates.question: Is the reading 'we were gentle' (epioi) or 'we were babes' (nepioi)?

sides.position: 'Gentle' (epioi) — Paul behaved with mildness, not apostolic authority (the received text)
'Babes' (nepioi) — Paul was as a harmless little child among them (Vulgate, Ethiopic, many witnesses)

themes: But we were gentle among you, even as a nurse cherisheth her children
Paul's gentleness like a nursing mother
the textual variant gentle vs babes
cherishing them as a mother her child

schools.summary: The depth of Paul's love: 'so being affectionately desirous of you (homeiromenoi — yearning over you, full of tender longing)' — his care no cold duty but warm, yearning affection; he was willing 'to have imparted (shared, given) unto you, not the gospel of God only, but also our own souls (our very selves, our lives)' — he would gladly have given not just the message but his own life for them; all this 'because ye were dear (beloved) unto us' — they had become so precious that he held nothing back, not even himself (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: we were willing to have imparted unto you, not the gospel of God only, but also our own souls
because ye were dear unto us
Paul's readiness to impart his very soul
self-giving love

schools.summary: Paul appeals to their memory of his hard work: 'ye remember our labour (kopos — toil) and travail (mochthos — hardship, weariness)' — the two strong words for exhausting work; Paul laboured with his own hands (tentmaking, Acts 18:3) to support himself, 'labouring night and day' (constant, wearying toil), 'because we would not be chargeable (a burden, a financial charge) unto any of you' — supporting himself precisely so that he might preach the gospel free of charge, putting no obstacle in their way (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Calvin: Paul laboured with his hands that he might preach without charge and remove every suspicion of greed; his self-support was a deliberate testimony to the freeness of the gospel he proclaimed.

themes: ye remember, brethren, our labour and travail: for labouring night and day
because we would not be chargeable unto any of you, we preached unto you the gospel of God
Paul's labour night and day not to burden them
the gospel preached free of charge

schools.summary: Paul calls both the Thessalonians and God to testify to his conduct: 'ye are witnesses, and God also' — they could vouch for his outward behaviour, God for his inward integrity; the three adverbs 'how holily (hosios — toward God, in piety and devotion), justly (dikaios — toward men, in righteousness and fairness), and unblameably (amemptos — without fault, giving no just ground for accusation) we behaved ourselves among you that believe' — his conduct pure toward God, righteous toward men, and blameless before all, his daily life open before the believers and matching his preaching (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Ye are witnesses, and God also, how holily and justly and unblameably we behaved ourselves
the holiness and blamelessness of Paul's conduct
holy toward God, just toward men, blameless before all
his walk confirming his preaching

schools.summary: Having likened himself to a nursing mother (v.7), Paul now takes the image of a father: 'as a father doth his children' — his care both maternal in tenderness and paternal in guidance; the three verbs of fatherly dealing — 'exhorted (parakalountes — urged, encouraged to duty), and comforted (paramythoumenoi — consoled, cheered the discouraged), and charged (martyromenoi — testified, solemnly enjoined)' — Paul exhorting to right conduct, comforting the fainthearted, and solemnly charging them; and this with 'every one of you' — each person individually, not the crowd in mass, with personal attention (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: how we exhorted and comforted and charged every one of you, as a father doth his children
Paul's fatherly dealing with each of them
exhorting, comforting, charging
individual, personal care

schools.summary: The purpose of all his fatherly exhortation: 'that ye would walk worthy of God' — that the Thessalonians might so live and conduct themselves as befits those who belong to God, a walk corresponding to their high calling; the lofty measure being not merely worthy of men or of the gospel but 'worthy of God' himself, matching his character and honour; 'who hath called (invited, summoned) you unto his kingdom and glory' — the believer called to share God's own kingdom and glory, this high destiny the motive for a worthy walk (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: That ye would walk worthy of God
who hath called you unto his kingdom and glory
the aim of Paul's care: a walk worthy of God
called to God's kingdom and glory

schools.summary: The cause of Paul's renewed thanksgiving: when the Thessalonians 'received the word of God which ye heard of us, ye received it not as the word of men (a mere human message) but as it is in truth, the word of God' — recognizing the gospel for what it truly is, not Paul's word but God's, and receiving it with the reverence due to God's own speech; 'which effectually worketh (energeitai — works mightily, is operative, produces its effect) also in you that believe' — the word so received being no dead letter but a living, working power, producing its saving effects in believers, the proof of right reception being that it works in them (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: ye received it not as the word of men, but as it is in truth, the word of God
which effectually worketh also in you that believe
the word received as the word of God
the word working mightily in believers

schools.summary: The Thessalonians 'became followers (imitators) of the churches of God which in Judaea are in Christ Jesus' — they came to resemble the first Jewish-Christian churches, not by deliberately copying them but by undergoing the same experience of persecution; 'for ye also have suffered like things of your own countrymen, even as they have of the Jews' — as the Judaean churches suffered from the unbelieving Jews, so the Thessalonian believers suffered from their own fellow-citizens (Acts 17:5), persecution from one's own people marking both (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: ye became followers of the churches of God which in Judaea are in Christ Jesus
ye also have suffered like things of your own countrymen, even as they have of the Jews
sharing the sufferings of the Judaean churches
persecution from their own countrymen

schools.summary: Paul's solemn indictment of the persecuting Jews (the unbelieving leaders and opponents, not the nation as such): they 'both killed the Lord Jesus, and their own prophets' — guilty of the supreme crime of slaying the Lord, and before that of killing the prophets God sent them (cf. Matthew 23:31, 37); 'and have persecuted us (exedioxan — drove us out, hunted the apostles)'; 'they please not God, and are contrary (hostile, opposed) to all men' — by their fierce opposition to the gospel's offer to the Gentiles, setting themselves against the good of all mankind (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Who both killed the Lord Jesus, and their own prophets, and have persecuted us
they please not God, and are contrary to all men
the indictment of those who killed the Lord and the prophets
opposing the gospel to the Gentiles

schools.summary: The specific charge: the persecutors were 'forbidding (hindering) us to speak to the Gentiles that they might be saved' — opposing the very preaching by which the Gentiles could be saved, the crowning sin of begrudging salvation to others; 'to fill up their sins alway' — by this opposition completing, bringing to the full measure, their sins, adding sin to sin until the measure of their iniquity was full (cf. Matthew 23:32; Genesis 15:16); 'for the wrath is come upon them to the uttermost (eis telos — at last, completely, or to the end)' — the divine judgment overtaking them, pointing to the impending destruction of Jerusalem (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Forbidding us to speak to the Gentiles that they might be saved, to fill up their sins alway
for the wrath is come upon them to the uttermost
filling up their sins and the wrath come
judgment on the persecutors

schools.summary: The strong word for the separation: 'being taken from you (aporphanisthentes — orphaned, bereaved, torn away as a parent from children) for a short time (a season) in presence (in body, face to face), not in heart' — Paul felt his enforced absence as a bereavement, though his heart remained with them; the separation only outward and brief, never parting them in love; he 'endeavoured the more abundantly (made every effort, with redoubled eagerness) to see your face with great desire (intense longing)' — the very separation deepening his yearning to return (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: being taken from you for a short time in presence, not in heart
endeavoured the more abundantly to see your face with great desire
Paul's longing to see them again
parted in presence but not in heart

schools.summary: Paul's earnest desire and attempts: 'we would have come unto you, even I Paul, once and again (more than once, repeatedly)' — he had tried more than once to return to Thessalonica, the singular 'I Paul' stressing his own personal longing, his attempts renewed efforts not a single passing wish (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: 'but Satan hindered (enekopsen — blocked, impeded, cut off, as breaking up a road) us' — some obstacle, whether persecution, hostile machinations, or circumstances, Paul attributes to Satan's opposition; the adversary blocks the work of the gospel and the fellowship of the saints.

themes: we would have come unto you, even I Paul, once and again; but Satan hindered us
Paul's thwarted attempts to come
hindered by Satan
the adversary blocking the gospel

schools.summary: Paul's affectionate rhetorical question: what is his 'hope, or joy, or crown of rejoicing (kauchesis — boasting, the victor's wreath of which one boasts)?' — what he most prizes and glories in; the warm answer, 'are not even ye?' — the Thessalonians themselves are his hope, joy, and crown, his converts being the reward and glory of his ministry, the wreath he will present 'in the presence of our Lord Jesus Christ at his coming (parousia)' (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Cyril: this glorying is reserved for the coming of the Lord, when Paul will present his converts before Christ; they will be his joy and crown in that great day of his appearing.

themes: what is our hope, or joy, or crown of rejoicing?
Are not even ye in the presence of our Lord Jesus Christ at his coming?
the Thessalonians Paul's hope, joy, and crown
his converts his glory at Christ's coming

schools.summary: Paul, having asked the question (v.19), now answers it plainly and emphatically: 'for ye are our glory and joy' — the Thessalonians are, without question, the apostle's glory (his honour and boast, the visible fruit of his labour, the proof of his ministry in which he glories) and his joy (the source of his deepest gladness, their faith and steadfastness filling his heart with rejoicing, now and supremely at Christ's coming) — Barnes, Gill, Clarke.

themes: For ye are our glory and joy
the Thessalonians as Paul's glory and joy
his honour and boast
the joy of his heart
